items.title: Dataset as a feature matrix
items.scenario: You have a tabular dataset with 10,000 customers and 20 numerical features per customer (age, income, spend, etc.). You store it as a 10000 x 20 matrix X, where each row is a customer and each column is a feature.
items.explanation: Treating the dataset as a matrix makes operations like centering columns, scaling features, or applying a linear model y = Xw straightforward. Matrix addition and scalar multiplication correspond to shifting and scaling entire columns or rows at once.

items.title: Grayscale image as a matrix
items.scenario: A grayscale image of size 28 x 28 (like MNIST digits) is represented as a 28 x 28 matrix of pixel intensities between 0 and 255.
items.explanation: Matrix operations can brighten, darken, or normalize the image by scaling or shifting all entries. Stacking many such matrices row-wise can create a larger data matrix for training computer vision models.

items.title: System of linear equations as a matrix equation
items.scenario: You want to solve the system: 2x + 3y = 5 and 4x - y = 1. You represent the coefficients as a 2 x 2 matrix A, variables as a 2 x 1 vector x, and right-hand side as a 2 x 1 vector b so that A x = b.
items.explanation: Writing systems as A x = b lets you use matrix operations (row operations, inverses, decompositions) to analyze and solve the system efficiently. This generalizes to large systems arising in optimization and ML.

items.title: Mini-batch in a neural network
items.scenario: In a neural network, a mini-batch of 64 examples with 128 features each is stored as a 64 x 128 matrix X. A weight matrix W of size 128 x 256 maps features to 256 hidden units.
items.explanation: Even before learning about full matrix multiplication, you can see that adding a bias vector or scaling features across the batch corresponds to simple matrix operations on X. The matrix viewpoint enables efficient batched computation on GPUs.
